Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: MAJOR CHANGE! Clicking on the g frame position (19, 12) caused all 5 green objects to disappear. A new purple/magenta object appeared at the bottom-left corner outside the grid. The g artifacts remain. This suggests the g frame was a "target" position, and clicking it triggered a transformation.
- Goal model: The purple object might be a player character or tool. Need to understand how to use it.
- Plan: Investigate the new purple object and try moving it with directional keys.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The UP action didn't move the purple object (center still at 57, 6). The purple object is outside the grid at the bottom-left. Let me reconsider what happened.
Looking at the image again, I see:
1. The grid has gray squares where green objects used to be (now they're g color with 12 pixels each - smaller than the original 16-pixel w tiles)
2. A purple character/object at the bottom-left
3. The g artifacts from before still exist
Wait, I see something important now. The positions where green objects were are now g-colored objects with 12 pixels (not 16 like normal w tiles). These are the "ghost" positions. And the original g artifacts (4 small 2-pixel objects) are still around.
Looking at the image more carefully, the purple object at the bottom-left looks like a little character with a white face. This might be a player character that I need to navigate into the grid.
But UP didn't move it. Let me try RIGHT since the grid is to the right of the purple object. Or maybe I need to click on the purple object first to select it, then use directional keys.
Actually, wait - let me reconsider the whole game flow:
1. Started with 5 green objects on a grid
2. Clicked on first green object -> got G border
3. Clicked on second green object -> G border moved, g artifacts appeared at (19, 12)
4. Clicked on g artifact position (19, 12) -> all green objects disappeared, purple character appeared
Maybe the game is about:
- Selecting objects to "collect" them
- The g artifacts mark a "spawn" or "target" position
- The purple character is now the player that needs to navigate somewhere
Let me try moving the purple character with RIGHT since the grid entrance is to the right.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: The purple object at (57, 6) didn't move with UP. Green objects were replaced by smaller g-colored objects (12 pixels each) at their old positions. The purple object appears to be a character outside the grid.
Plan: Try RIGHT to move the purple character toward the grid entrance. Also try clicking on the purple object to select it first.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The purple object is at center (57, 6) with boundary around rows 52-63, cols 3-10
# The grid starts around row 11, col 11
# The purple object is below and to the left of the grid

# Try RIGHT to move toward the grid
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"RIGHT result: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
purple = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p']
if purple:
    n = purple[0]
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"Purple center after RIGHT: {center}, boundary={n['boundary'][:6]}")

# Also check if the g objects changed
g_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'g']
print(f"g objects: {len(g_nodes)}")
for n in g_nodes:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
